However, this is good news for artists and writers who rely on their imagination.</p><h2>Virgo (Aug. 23-Sept. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Be careful because there's a moon alert in effect all day today, taking place in your money house, which means don't lend money, don't spend money, don't make financial decisions because later, you could have regrets. (You can buy food and gas.)</p><h2>Libra (Sept. 23-Oct. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> There's a moon alert all day in your sign, making you indecisive and unsure of what to do. The tricky thing is if you make an important decision, you might later regret it. Instead, use this energy to socialize, relax and explore your creative talents.</p><h2>Scorpio (Oct. 23-Nov. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> There are four planets urging you to explore your creativity. It's also a good time to enjoy playful activities with kids and social outings. Nevertheless, because the moon is hiding in your chart, you might want to keep a low profile. Your call.</p><h2>Sagittarius (Nov. 22-Dec. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is a good day to schmooze with friends and enjoy the company of groups. However, do not commit to any important mandate or line of action because there is a moon alert happening all day.</p><h2>Capricorn (Dec. 22-Jan. 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> When talking to parents, bosses, VIPs or the police today, be careful because today is ripe for dubious decisions. It's not the day to ask for permission or approval. Wait until tomorrow. Today, you want to steer clear of important demands.</p><h2>Aquarius (Jan. 20-Feb. 18)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You might feel the wanderlust today, which is why travel or making travel plans will appeal. Don't spend money or commit to travel plans today. Wait until tomorrow. However, you can do your homework and get information and explore ideas because you will do so with a fresh, original approach.</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Be careful because with Mars in your sign, you're keen to act and defend yourself. However, today there's a moon alert in effect all day, and it's taking place in one of your money houses, which means it's a poor day to make decisions about inheritances, taxes, debt or shared property. Do nothing.</p><h2>If today is your birthday</h2><p>Sculptor, painter, architect, and poet Michelangelo (1475) shares your birthday today. You are an idealist with a quest for perfection. You are motivated, tenacious, imaginative, and sensitive. This is a year of new beginnings, adventures, and major changes in your life. You're beginning a new cycle, which means it's time to take action and define your goals. Prepare for leadership. </p>
entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: So long, Rev. Jackson. Details on Friday’s homecoming service
entries.summary: <p>Today, let us all remember, as the Rev. Jesse Jackson famously said, to “Keep Hope Alive.”</p><p>A homecoming service to honor Jackson, the civil rights icon and two-time presidential candidate who died on Feb. 17, will feature three former presidents and thousands attending the memorial tribute Friday at a giant arena in Chicago’s Pullman neighborhood. The headquarters of Jackson’s Rainbow PUSH Coalition, 930 E. 50th St., was not large enough to host the event.</p><p><b>WHERE WILL THE SERVICE BE HELD?</b> At the House of Hope Convention and Event Center, 752 E. 114th St. The House of Hope arena has about 10,000 seats.</p><p><b>CAN I GO?</b> Yes. Get there early. Doors open to the public at 9 a.m. Services begin at 11 a.m. Parking is limited, but <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago/2026/03/04/jesse-jackson-service-house-hope-travel-transit" >several public transit options are available.</a></p><p><b>HOW CAN I FOLLOW ALONG, WATCH OR LISTEN TO THE SERVICE?</b> The service will be carried by WBEZ 91.5 FM starting at 11 a.m. WBEZ’s Sasha-Ann Simons will co-host live coverage with journalist and author Natalie Moore. Additionally, the Sun-Times will have several reporters live blogging the event on its homepage. You can also find livestreams at<a class="Link" href="http://jessejacksonlegacy.com" target="_blank" ><u> JesseJacksonLegacy.com</u></a>, CSPAN and other outlets. Expect a very long service.</p><p><b>WHO ARE THE OFFICIANTS?</b> Dr. Charles Jenkins, Pastor Emeritus at the Fellowship Missionary Baptist Church (now known as Fellowship Chicago), and Rev. James T. Meeks, the founder and pastor emeritus of Salem Baptist Church of Chicago. Jackson’s connections to Fellowship run deep. In 1965, Fellowship’s Rev. Clay Evans ordained Rev. Jackson as an associate minister of the church.</p><p><b>WHO ARE THE BIGGEST NAMES SPEAKING? </b>This service will also be part of presidential history, since it is rare for past presidents to be together. Former Presidents Barack Obama, Bill Clinton and Joe Biden will be among the speakers. President Trump and ex-President George W. Bush were invited, but are not attending. Former Vice President Kamala Harris is also speaking. Former first ladies Hillary Clinton and Jill Biden will attend. Former first lady Michelle Obama, a South Side native who grew up with Sanita Jackson is not on the list. The Sun-Times asked the Obama team why she is not attending and so far have not gotten a reply.</p><p><b>WHO ARE OTHER NOTABLE SPEAKERS?</b> Mayor Brandon Johnson; Gov JB Pritzker; Rep. Maxine Waters,D-Calif.; Chicago Cubs chairman Tom Ricketts; Founder, Chairman, and Chief Executive Officer of Loop Capital, Jim Reynolds; Judge Greg Mathis; Chair of the Rainbow PUSH Coalition, C.K. Hoffler, NBA star Isaiah Thomas; National Action Network founder Rev. Al Sharpton; Arab American Institute founder James Zogby, the President of Columbia, Gustave Francisco Petro Urrego and Rep. Jesus “Chuy” Garcia, D-Ill.</p><p><b>WHO ARE AMONG THOSE OFFERING PRAYERS?</b> Rabbi Sharon Brous, founder of IKAR, a Los Angeles synagogue; Father Michael Pfleger from St. Sabina’s and Rev. Otis Moss III, from Trinity United Church of Christ.</p><p><b>WHAT JACKSON FAMILY MEMBERS ARE PART OF THE SERVICE?</b> Sons Yusef; former Rep. Jesse Jackson Jr.; Rep. Jonathan Jackson; daughter, Sanita, who will sing; daughter Ashley and Jackson’s wife, Jacqueline.</p><p><b>WHO WILL BE PERFORMING? </b>Chicago’s very own Jennifer Hudson – who has won an Emmy, Grammy, Oscar, and Tony – and Gospel legends Bebe and Marvin Winans will perform.</p><p><b>WHAT IS THE SATURDAY EVENT? </b>It’s a smaller service at Rainbow-PUSH headquarters, starting at 10 a.m. that is expected to run until 1:30 or 2 p.m. Performers will include Stevie Wonder.</p><p><b>WHEN AND WHERE WILL REV. JACKSON BE BURIED?</b> After the Saturday service there will be a procession to Oak Woods Cemetery, 1035 E 67th St. Sun-Times columnist Lee Bey <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/architecture-design/2026/03/05/rev-jesse-jackson-laid-rest-oak-woods-cemetery" >has a great read</a> about this historic burial place.</p>
entries.author: Lynn Sweet

entries.title: Bulls avoid that losing feeling for a night, but it's still taking toll
entries.summary: <p>PHOENIX — There was a lot of losing under Joe Mullaney his first few years at Providence College, but once the former coach retired and Rick Pitino took over, a young guard named Billy Donovan forgot what losing basketball looked like.</p><p>And not for the short term, either.</p><p>The rest of his college career, his time as an assistant coach, and then once Donovan finally sat in the coaching seat himself, winning season after winning season. Sure, there were a few down years every now and then, but both in college and the NBA, Donovan and winning were synonymous.</p><p>Leave it to the Bulls to be the nightmarish reminder of his early Providence days, and that’s even with a somewhat impressive 105-103 victory over the Suns on Thursday.</p><p>“No, and I want to win,” Donovan insisted when reminded that he’s never really lost like he has the last six weeks as a player or coach. “You’re competing to try and win, so absolutely you always want to do a good job and you want to be in position to win basketball games. I’ve said this many times, it’s a result-oriented business. With that being said, I feel like I have a job to do with the current roster and the guys that are here.”</p><p>Even the guys that aren’t “here” long-term.</p><p>That’s the tricky part in all of this. Since the roster purge at the trade deadline, the Bulls’ front office did rid itself of seven free-agent contracts but also took in players in walk years. Anfernee Simons, Collin Sexton, Guerschon Yabusele and Nick Richards could all be wearing different uniforms by July, while Donovan and the front office have to make decisions on restricted free agent Jaden Ivey, as well as decide if second-year guard Rob Dillingham has the potential to live up to his lottery pick status from the 2024 draft.</p><p>That’s where Donovan is trying to keep his focus, evident by Sexton scoring 30 points in the victory, Yabusele scoring 16, including a clutch corner three late, and Richards chipping in with nine points and 11 rebounds against his former team. Dillingham also had some flash moments with 11 points in 20 minutes.</p><p>“Hopefully, I can help a guy like a Rob Dillingham, who hasn’t had a much of an opportunity,” Donovan said. “Same with Lenny [Miller] and some of these other guys. Can I help Matas [Buzelis] grow? Not only am I going through it personally, but the team is different for Josh [Giddey], Matas, Patrick [Williams] and Jalen [Smith], and those guys, too. We’ve got to lean into whatever the situation is.”</p><p>That has been the ongoing message from Donovan, especially after the Bulls went 0-11 in February and still came out of the game against the Suns losers of 15 of their last 18 games. He wants his players to focus on what they can control.</p><p>That’s easy to ask of a veteran such as Isaac Okoro, who battled with the Suns’ Devin Booker all night.</p><p>“I’ve always been an advocate of if you’re trying to do too much, doing what you’re not used to doing or doing stuff out of the ordinary, it doesn’t look good,” Okoro said. “People can tell by watching you play. Like if I was coming out here and going between the legs, step-back threes, it would just look out of the ordinary.</p><p>“I’ve been able to be in the league six years because of the role and the player I am of trying to take the toughest assignment every single night, being a hard-nosed player, put my body on the line. I’ve always kept that mindset whether we have a full roster or half a roster.”<br></p>
entries.author: Joe Cowley

entries.title: Bulls hope to have answers about guard Jaden Ivey sooner than later
entries.summary: <p>PHOENIX — A lot of questions could be answered about <b>Jaden Ivey</b> in the next nine days as the guard enters a crucial part of his knee-strengthening program back in Chicago.</p><p>The obvious one? Will the Bulls see him on the court again this season?</p><p>Coach <b>Billy Donovan</b> is holding out hope that the answer is yes, but he reiterated that there will be frequent daily phone calls between the members of the Bulls’ medical staff with the team on this 10-day road trip and the ones working with Ivey at the Advocate Center.</p><p>“That’s going to be really, really critical,” Donovan said Thursday of Ivey’s time away from the team. “He’s made strides and he’s gotten better, but the medical guys want to see some improvement in that strengthening. Two weeks passed a few days ago, now he’s got this third week, and he’s gotten stronger, but to make another jump there are certain things the medical guys are going to want to see and have him cross some thresholds.”</p><p>Ivey had a horrific setback to his career last season with the Pistons, breaking his left fibula Jan. 1, 2025. He attempted to come back this season with the Pistons and was in the rotation when the Bulls acquired him, but by all accounts he was not nearly the same explosive player he was pre-injury.</p><p>It took Donovan four games into Ivey’s Bulls career to notice that.</p><p>So after he complained about knee soreness, the medical staff decided to shut him down for a few weeks and work on strengthening the area around the knee. It’s now on Week 3, and the runway to returning this season is getting shorter.</p><p>Because Ivey is a restricted free agent, the Bulls would love to get more eyes on him on the court, but there’s a wait-and-see to that. One thing Donovan was asked was if there were concerns that the guard is dealing with a mental aspect from the injury and just doesn’t trust the leg?</p><p>“I’ve never got that from him,” Donovan said. “He ended up going out and playing in Detroit. We could tell that in just the four games he was here he wasn’t moving great, and then he complained that his knee got sore, but I never got the impression that he was playing worried about it. I just felt like he wasn’t moving well.”</p><h3>Street clothes</h3><p>It was a crowded bench for the Bulls in Phoenix, and unfortunately half the bodies were in street clothes: <b>Matas Buzelis </b>(ankle), <b>Josh Giddey </b>(right ankle), <b>Patrick Williams</b> (quad), <b>Jalen Smith </b>(calf strain) and <b>Anfernee Simons</b> (left hand).</p><p>The good news was that Donovan felt like help was coming when the team plays in Sacramento, where Giddey, Buzelis and Williams are expected to return.</p><p>Simons and Smith are still working through their injuries with no timetable.</p><h3>Down under</h3><p>Second-round pick <b>Lachlan Olbrich </b>played in his 21st game Thursday, and the key for the 6-8 Aussie in his rookie season has been not to have expectations.</p><p>“I didn’t know what to expect coming into it, obviously, but I feel like I built a really strong foundation,” Olbrich said. “I’ve been talking a lot about foundation and hopefully in the next couple seasons I can really build on it.</p><p>“That’s really been the focus. I’m not worried about the minutes or the games played, anything like that. It’s just been a massive learning experience for me in my first year, which has been good for me.”<br></p>
entries.author: Joe Cowley

entries.title: White Sox right-hander Drew Thorpe's bullpen session goes off without a hitch
entries.summary: <p>GLENDALE, Ariz. — <b>Drew Thorpe</b> had a smile on his face Thursday in the Sox’ clubhouse at Camelback Ranch. He’s less than 12 months removed from Tommy John surgery on his right elbow and threw 20 pitches, all fastballs, off a mound Tuesday at Camelback Ranch without incident.</p><p>It was his first time on a mound since the surgery and after a pre-camp setback that caused him to back off his rehab progression.</p><p>“My bullpen was good on Tuesday,” he said hours before the Sox played the Guardians on Thursday night. “Looking at the next day, it was more beneficial just to see where I was at. I was pretty sore, which is to be expected, and I feel a lot better today.”</p><p>Thorpe said he was throwing long toss before the opening of spring training when he started feeling some tenderness in his surgically repaired elbow. At that point, he backed off throwing a bit. The normal recovery and rehab progression from T.J. surgery to actively throwing in a game is 12-14 months and sometimes 18.</p><p>The shorter the rehab period, the better the chances of recurrence and a second surgery.</p><p>Thorpe was concerned enough that he went for a consultation with <b>Dr. Keith Meister</b>, who performed the surgery. Meister also removed a bone spur from the elbow in September 2024. Meister cleared Thorpe to continue throwing.</p><p>“Obviously, that was great. There’s nothing wrong in there,” Thorpe said. “It gave me peace of mind. Just being able to build from there has been good for me.”</p><p>Thorpe will continue to increase his capacity throwing off a mound and is expected to do so again Friday. He hasn’t pitched in a game since July 31, 2024.</p><p>“He continues to progress,” Sox manager <b>Will Venable</b> said. “Again, we’re just trying to be mindful of his slow build up here and make sure he’s healthy. He’s trending in the right direction.”</p><h3>More injury updates</h3><p>As promised, <b>Andrew Benintendi</b> was back in the lineup against the Guardians, batting fifth and playing left field. He complained about overall soreness in his right side and has only played in three February spring games with two hits in seven at bats, both doubles.</p><p>“I’m excited to get this going,” Venable said. “We’ve just been slow-playing this thing to be cautious.”</p><p>Benintendi played in only 116 games last season because of a host of injuries, starting when he was hit by a pitch during spring training and suffered a non-displaced fracture in his right hand. That was followed by a left abductor strain and tendinitis in his left Achilles tendon. No wonder the Sox were being cautious.</p><p>The Sox have also monitored outfielder <b>Everson Pereria</b> since he felt soreness in his right oblique while taking swings last week off a Trajekt machine. Venable said there’s no timetable for getting him back into game action.</p><p>“We are looking at next week to get him out there if he continues to progress,” Venable added. “In some sort of game.”</p><p>Pitcher <b>Wikelman Gonzalez</b> left the game Tuesday in the seventh inning with tightness in his lower back.</p><h3>Guardians 12, Sox 3</h3><p><b>Sean Burke</b> had a poor start to open the game, allowing four runs and four hits to the first six batters he faced. <b>Angel Martinez</b>, <b>Brayan Rocchio</b> and <b>George Valera</b> opened with doubles. With two outs, <b>CJ Kayfus</b> hit a two-run homer. After the rough start, Burke retired seven of the last eight he faced through three innings. “Last year when things went sideways for me early, I had some games that I wanted back,” Burke said. “I got in some trouble in the early innings, and I let that affect me. But tonight, I gave up that four-spot early and kept my head in the game. If it’s the regular season, I’d still be giving my team a chance to win.”</p><p>• <b>Luisangel Acuna </b>was hit by a <b>Tyler Bibee</b> pitch to lead off the first but seemed to suffer no ill effects.</p><p>• <b>Andrew Benintendi</b> was back in the lineup as projected and flied out to center in the second. He struck out swinging to lead off the fifth and came out of the game.</p><p>• <b>On deck: </b>Sox at Diamondbacks, 2:10 p.m. Friday, Scottsdale, <b>Anthony Kay</b> vs. <b>Daniel Eagen</b>.<br></p>
entries.author: Barry M. Bloom

entries.title: Fox 32 Chicago fires anchor Scott Schneider, executive producer Marissa Rubino in latest moves
entries.summary: <p>Fox 32 fired anchor Scott Schneider and executive producer Marissa Rubino on Thursday night, the Sun-Times has learned, the latest in a series of changes at the station.</p><p>Though sources were unaware of the reason for the dismissals, they followed the hiring of four reporters and a producer in the last month. Fox 32 vice president of news content and programming Sean O’Heir appears to be remaking the newsroom since taking the title in September. Schneider, who anchored the 5 and 9 p.m. newscasts, had been with the station for 10 years, and Rubino for almost seven. Messages left for Fox Television Stations' communications department weren't returned. </p><p>“Marissa was an extremely hard-working and dedicated news producer who wore so many hats in the newsroom,” a source said. “It will be very hard to replace her.”</p><p>Fox 32 is still without a lead sports anchor after Lou Canellis left for NBC 5 in late January. Cassie Carlson and Tina Nguyen appear to be sharing the role while the station seeks a potential hire.</p><p>It aleady has been a volatile year for Chicago TV newscasts. NBC 5 fired sports anchor Mike Berman in January before hiring Canellis. Last month, WGN let go of nine on-air talents and several behind-the-scenes staffers as part of broadcaster Nexstar's nationwide cuts. Now Fox 32 is making changes.<br></p>
entries.author: Jeff Agrest

entries.title: From childhood through my journalism career, Jesse Jackson remained a commanding force
entries.summary: <p>I was a teen when I first met the Rev. Jesse Jackson. Well, I didn’t really meet him, I just entered his civil rights orbit, not knowing this was the man the <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/obituary/2026/02/17/jesse-jackson-embodied-era-civil-rights-leadership-natalie-moore" target="_blank" >Black community</a> would call to challenge racism, inequity and lack of representation for the next five decades.</p><p>It was late '70s. My parents had moved us from the Near South Side to southwest suburban Woodridge, enrolling us kids at a then-predominantly white high school.</p><p>Black families were just beginning to integrate the area, and Downers Grove South High was experiencing racial strife. A girl named Tina moved there from the South Side, and this new friend couldn’t hang out on weekends because her grandmother dragged her to Operation PUSH every Saturday.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-1e0000" name="image-1e0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ccc201d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/702x394+0+37/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2c%2F17%2F2df42c484e1a9894e6ccf5801d8e%2F3-w-jesse-at-per-so-na-lounge-december-2018-1.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/96eece3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/702x394+0+37/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2c%2F17%2F2df42c484e1a9894e6ccf5801d8e%2F3-w-jesse-at-per-so-na-lounge-december-2018-1.jpeg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/510aff4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/702x394+0+37/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2c%2F17%2F2df42c484e1a9894e6ccf5801d8e%2F3-w-jesse-at-per-so-na-lounge-december-2018-1.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Rev. Jesse Jackson addresses National Association of Black Journalists-Chicago Chapter members at the group's 2018 holday fundraiser as chapter President Maudlyne Ihejirika looks on." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/510aff4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/702x394+0+37/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2c%2F17%2F2df42c484e1a9894e6ccf5801d8e%2F3-w-jesse-at-per-so-na-lounge-december-2018-1.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a7b5856/2147483647/strip/true/crop/702x394+0+37/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2c%2F17%2F2df42c484e1a9894e6ccf5801d8e%2F3-w-jesse-at-per-so-na-lounge-december-2018-1.jpeg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/510aff4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/702x394+0+37/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2c%2F17%2F2df42c484e1a9894e6ccf5801d8e%2F3-w-jesse-at-per-so-na-lounge-december-2018-1.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Rev. Jesse Jackson addresses National Association of Black Journalists-Chicago Chapter members at the group’s 2018 holday fundraiser as chapter President Maudlyne Ihejirika looks on.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Her granny invited us to come along, saying with the strife at the school, it would be good for us kids to hear from this civil rights leader battling racism and discrimination. Tina said there were cute boys there. Off we went.</p><p>Our other friends eventually peeled off, but every Saturday until Tina moved, I was listening intently to Jackson assuring me that <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/columnists/2026/02/18/lights-camera-jesse-jackson-civil-rights-leader-pop-culture-presence" >“I Am Somebody!”</a></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-de0000" name="module-de0000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Opinion bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Opinion</h2></div> </div><p>Fast forward. It’s 1984. I’m attending the predominantly white University of Iowa, and Jackson is the second Black candidate ever to run for U.S. president. He’s winning <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2026/02/17/rev-jesse-jackson-legacy-chicago-democrats" >statewide primaries and caucuses</a> — the first Black candidate to do so.</p><p>I remember watching his campaign speeches with some of our small, tight-knit Black student population, jokingly chanting his slogan, "Run, Jesse, Run," while believing he didn’t have a snowball’s chance. But the joke was on us. He finished third in the Democratic primary, garnering 18% of the popular vote and registering 1 million new voters.</p><p>It’s 1987 now. I finish grad school, get my dream job in journalism and am watching Jackson run again as a major contender for the 1988 election. He registers a whopping 7 million new voters and builds a "Rainbow Coalition" that propels him to rarefied air — a second-place finish in the primary that put respect on his name. His famous "Keep Hope Alive" speech at the Democratic National Convention brings me and my Black journalist friends watching together nearly to tears.</p><p>Before and after that historic campaign, throughout the '80s and '90s, there were no beats I was assigned where I didn’t encounter the man our Black community simply called "Jesse." On the race and social justice and urban affairs beats, he was the required interview. But he also was a constant on <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2026/02/21/how-jesse-jackson-shaped-chicago-education-from-protesting-school-segregation-to-reopening-dyett-hs" >education</a> or politics, housing and policing, business and finance, nonprofits, sports — always there agitating, demanding equity and insisting economic justice was absolutely social justice.</p><h3></h3><h3>Unrelenting activism</h3><p>Lots of our Black middle class owed corporate climbs and successful entrepreneurship to his unrelenting activism — Rainbow PUSH boycotts forcing corporations to open exclusionary jobs and contracts, distributorships, franchises and board memberships. In those days, no company wanted to see Jesse leading a picket line outside their offices. His successful actions against Ford Motor Co., Anheuser-Busch, Major League Baseball, Nike and Mitsubishi are just a few I followed and wrote about.</p><p>Then there was his international diplomacy. No one could bring ’em home like Jesse could as he traveled the globe, securing freedom for U.S. servicemen, international political prisoners and hostages. I was glued to news feeds following Jesse with U.S. Navy Lt. Robert Goodman, captured in Syria, in 1983; and U.S. Army Sgts. Andrew Ramirez and Christopher Stone, along with Spc. Steven Gonzales, captured in Yugoslavia, in 1999. Soon after, I erupted in cheers with everyone else the moments Jesse landed with them on American soil.</p><p>His advocacy extended to my industry, which historically has little problem highlighting inequity unless fingers are pointed at it. Knowing it mattered in coverage of our community, he fiercely championed newsroom diversity. Many legendary Chicago journalists had jobs saved or promotions fueled by discreet letters or phone calls from Jesse, or picketing. As I was graduating from college, he was leading an ’85 boycott of WBBM-TV<b>, </b>Channel 2, after the city’s only Black anchor, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/entertainment-and-culture/2023/10/23/23929037/harry-porterfield-dead-television-news-anchor-chicago-obituary" >Harry Porterfield</a>, was replaced when the station brought back Bill Kurtis to reunite him with Walter Jacobson. The boycott led to the historic hiring of the first Black general manager of a network-owned station — and a young <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/movies-and-tv/2025/02/24/lester-holt-leave-nbc-nightly-news-dateline-chicago" >Lester Holt</a> who succeeded the duo years later.</p><p>What the Black community knew was that when racial trouble came, you called Jesse.<b> </b>We knew societal powers-that-be did not want to contend with this civil rights leader and the public excoriation he and Rainbow PUSH brought. Through the <a class="Link" href="https://nabjonline.org/" target="_blank" >National Association of Black Journalists</a> at the national level, and as a longtime officer of the <a class="Link" href="https://nabjchicago.org/" target="_blank" >NABJ-Chicago chapter</a>, we partnered for decades with Jesse on issues of newsroom inequity and coverage bias.</p><p>When his personal troubles came to light, initial judgment faded quickly in Chicago’s Black community. We were forgiving, in a "He who is without sin …" kind of way, accepting Jesse as a complicated figure — as history’s brilliant and famous usually are, a little messiness to be found in all closets. Certainly, it was so with his mentor, the Rev. Martin Luther King Jr., whose calling changed the trajectory of a nation. And so it was with Jesse, last of the great civil rights icons, whom history will measure by his calling that continued well into his debilitating illness.</p><p>Invited to visit him at home those final weeks, a Jackson family friend said she chose not to, preferring to remember the 1970s, afro-haired, dashiki-garbed, rafters-shaking orator who mesmerized an army of volunteers when Rainbow PUSH was still on 79th Street, before moving to 930 E. 50th St.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-floatRight> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-2f0000" name="image-2f0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/827ad70/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2100x1179+0+111/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc0%2F57%2F65c5d7574f1eaad64c9f9e323b28%2Fjessejackson-vertical.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b4457a5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2100x1179+0+111/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc0%2F57%2F65c5d7574f1eaad64c9f9e323b28%2Fjessejackson-vertical.jpg There is a woman in the middle and two men in suits at her side. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/78e20b2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2100x1179+0+111/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc0%2F57%2F65c5d7574f1eaad64c9f9e323b28%2Fjessejackson-vertical.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ffdf907/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2100x1179+0+111/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc0%2F57%2F65c5d7574f1eaad64c9f9e323b28%2Fjessejackson-vertical.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/78e20b2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2100x1179+0+111/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc0%2F57%2F65c5d7574f1eaad64c9f9e323b28%2Fjessejackson-vertical.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The Rev. Jesse Jackson, author Maudlyne Ihejirika and attorney Johnnie Cochran.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided </p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>As for me, I'll remember the man who took me under his wings as the rookie lone reporter covering Rainbow PUSH every Saturday morning in the early ’90s, giving me scoops that raised my profile, inspired other outlets to start showing up and seeded a decades-long journalist-source relationship. My archive of stories of and photos with Jesse are immense, likely the same for <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/columnists/2026/02/17/after-covering-jesse-jackson-50-years-im-going-to-miss-fierce-activism-ebony" >many Black journalists</a> of my generation. One sticks out, however, and I was driven to find and share it the day he died. It was the late '90s. O.J. Simpson had been found not guilty of the 1994 murder of ex-wife Nicole Brown Simpson and her friend Ronald Goldman, after lawyer Johnnie Cochran famously stated in his ’95 trial, "If it doesn't fit, you must acquit." </p><p>Half the country applauded the verdict; the other half abhorred it. </p><p>Cochran shot to fame, making plans to set up shop in cities including Chicago. It was a weekend. I got a call from Jesse telling me to get down to Midway Airport right away.</p><p>"Why?" I stammered. "Do you want an exclusive with <a class="Link" href="https://www.cochranfirm.com/johnnie-cochran/" target="_blank" >Johnnie Cochran</a> or don't you?" Jesse asked.</p><p>I hightailed it to Midway, met and interviewed the legendary Cochran and wrote a story that was picked up nationally. I’m grinning like a Cheshire cat in the photo at the scoop I'd just gotten. That was the Jesse I knew, the Jesse I’ll remember. </p><p>Now when racial trouble comes, who we gonna call?</p><p><i>Maudlyne Ihejirika is the director of Journalism & Storytelling at the Field Foundation of Illinois. She worked at the Sun-Times for 29 years.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-a00000" name="module-a00000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Maudlyne Ihejirika

entries.title: Dear Abby: Mom is on the hook for estranged son's college loan
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> My son took out a couple of education loans, which I cosigned when he was starting college 10 years ago. A couple of years ago, I found out (from someone else) that he never finished college. When I confronted him, he mentioned that he "intends" to finish college and is working toward it. He did not mention how many credits he has completed, what made him quit or why he didn't consult me before dropping out. Shortly after that conversation, he stopped talking to or visiting me for a different reason. We haven't seen each other in two years.</p><p>Recently, I received a notice from a debt collector regarding the loan. I tried to contact my son to figure out what he plans to do about the payments, but to no avail. He has always had terrible money habits. Until he stopped talking to me, he relied on me to rescue him whenever he got into money trouble. I had to pay off another of his education loans when he started defaulting a few years back.</p><p>Because of all of this, he owes me a significant amount of money. I am at an age where it is important that I build a retirement fund. If I have to pay off this loan, it will put a big dent into my savings. A few people have recommended I take legal action against him. I am, however, reluctant to do so for fear of severing my relationship with him forever. Is there a less aggressive way to have him take accountability for this loan? — <i>MOM ON THE HOOK</i></p><p><b>DEAR MOM:</b> Face it, Mom. The son you have bailed out repeatedly is a deadbeat. He is avoiding you because he has no intention of paying back the money for which you so caringly cosigned 10 years ago. Contact an attorney and see what your options may be. Doing that is not aggressive or punitive. It may give you a road map to pull yourself out of this hole.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I recently saw a TV commercial in which a family of four was sitting at a table in a restaurant. The two kids were watching their parents text on their phones instead of socializing with each other and making pleasant conversation. It made me furious. Why? I was taught that it's disrespectful not to give people your exclusive, undivided attention and that there is a time and a place for everything. I think it's one of the reasons why so many people today lack appropriate social skills. Do you agree? — <i>PRESENT IN RHODE ISLAND</i></p><p><b>DEAR PRESENT:</b> I agree with you 100%. What you saw in that commercial was a textbook example of lazy parenting. You cannot teach young people communication skills without modeling them. This has been a subject of concern for educators and behavioral specialists for at least 30 years. The result has been two generations of adults who have trouble making eye contact when trying to relate with others.</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>For everything you need to know about wedding planning, order "How to Have a Lovely Wedding." Send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds) to: Dear Abby, Wedding Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: Don't punish TSA agents for Homeland Security's transgressions
entries.summary: <p>A friend of mine is a Transportation Security Administration agent. We had a very interesting conversation when I called him to check on how he was doing. He is OK, he said. </p><p>But his heart breaks for some of his co-workers: the single mom who just bought a house and now can’t meet her mortgage payment; the co-worker who may be evicted; the co-worker whose transmission went out and now cannot come to work because repairs are too expensive; co-workers who have medical issues; people who wonder if they can afford gas or food and are now looking at food pantries as an option.</p><p>Yes, U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement's actions are abhorrent, and we need to take a strong stand, raise our voices, vote, protest and expect Congress to stand up to the Department of Homeland Security for the inhumane treatment of undocumented immigrants.</p><p>But don't forget the plight of <a class="Link" href="https://thehill.com/homenews/senate/5759740-government-shutdown-tsa-funding/" target="_blank" >TSA agents</a>, who've also being unjustly targeted by the partial government shutdown due to an impasse over funding DHS. These TSA agents have nothing to do with ICE, and yet they are being penalized. We need to find a better solution. These American citizens do not deserve to be the scapegoat in this showdown.</p><p><i>Elizabeth Butler Marren, Beverly</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-220000" name="module-220000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>Give us your take</h3><br>Send letters to the editor to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. To be considered for publication, letters must include your full name, your neighborhood or hometown and a phone number for verification purposes. Letters should be a maximum of approximately 375 words.</div> </div><p></p><h3>More election guidance, please</h3><p>Thank you for the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/graphics/voter-guide-primary-election-illinois/" target="_blank" >2026 Illinois Primary Elections Voters Guide</a> in Sunday's Sun-Times. It’s a great summary of our choices in this primary election. However, I want more. One suggestion is that you could publish in-depth interviews with the candidates. </p><p>Also, when I lived in San Francisco, for every election, primary or general, I would get a thick paper booklet in the mail with statements from each candidate. As long as the candidate submitted the statement on time, within the word limits and without vulgarity, the Board of Elections would print their statement verbatim. This made it so much easier to research candidates. Because it was not produced by a specific party or candidate, it was comprehensive and helpful. Perhaps the Sun-Times could coordinate such a publication for future elections?</p><p><i>Barry Charles Saiff, South Loop</i></p><h3>Good news</h3><p>Saturday's Sun-Times made my day with its <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/music/2026/02/25/buddy-guy-sinners-movie-ryan-coogler-blues-music" >front page cover</a> of blues man Buddy Guy. Then, I turned to Page 9 to read an incredible story about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/obituaries/2026/02/25/john-sharkey-renowned-naperville-karate-instructor-whose-students-have-gone-on-to-hollywood-dies-at-69" >John Sharkey</a>, a karate teacher, who was taken too young. He devoted his life to karate with the help of his father. It is good to read heartwarming stories instead of those about war and hate, both downers. Thank you Sun-Times.</p><p><i>Virginia Dare McGraw, Naperville</i></p><h3>Foreign fiasco</h3><p>Donald Trump has opened a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/nation-world/2026/02/28/iran-trump-israel" >global can of worms</a> without a clue about what comes next. Trump doesn't plan past his next Diet Coke. This is what happens when you elect a repugnant, ignoramus president. Thanks, America. </p><p><i>Michael Shepherd, Bellwood</i></p><h3></h3>
entries.author: Letters to the Editor

entries.title: A rabbi accused of sexually abusing roommate as a teen is now teaching at Orthodox Jewish school in Chicago
entries.summary: <p>An Orthodox Jewish rabbi who was accused in a lawsuit of repeatedly sexually assaulting his high school roommate years ago while attending an East Coast boarding school is now a teacher at a Jewish school for boys on Chicago’s North Side, according to interviews and public records.</p><p>The rabbi works at Yeshiva Eitz Chaim at 6045 N. Keystone Ave., and was hired there several years ago as the sexual misconduct lawsuit was pending, according to court records from New York and Cook County.</p><p>The Chicago Sun-Times isn’t naming the rabbi because he has not been charged with a crime, and he and the accuser were minors when the alleged misconduct occurred.</p><p>Both are now adults, and their respective lawyers each declined to comment.</p><p>Filed in 2021, that lawsuit was settled this past fall quietly for undisclosed terms — confidentiality generally frowned upon by victim advocates, and restricted by other faith groups such as the Catholic church because such secrecy was used for years to hide legitimate claims and allow abuse to fester.</p><p>Typically such settlements come without an admission of wrongdoing.</p><p>Whether leaders of Yeshiva Eitz Chaim knew of the allegations when they hired the rabbi isn’t clear.</p><p>But school officials certainly have known about the accusations — which date to the early 2000s when the future rabbi and his accuser were students and dorm mates at Torah High School in Long Beach, N.Y. — for at least a year and continued to employ him, according to records and interviews.</p><p>Chicago attorney Hal M. Garfinkel released a statement Thursday from school leaders that said:</p><p>"Despite the civil claim being unsubstantiated, the School, out of an abundance of caution for the safety of its students, sought legal, rabbinical and professional guidance as to the appropriate next steps."</p><p>"After doing extensive independent research, they unanimously advised the School leadership that there is no cause for concern. The School proceeded to notify parents, lay leaders and supporters as to the civil suit along with the School's reaction and subsequent decision. They all wholeheartedly supported the School in its handling of the situation."</p><p>But school officials wouldn't say when parents were notified or provide details on their research, including who conducted it and how recently was it done.</p><p>Yeshiva Eitz Chaim’s web site says the school “provides both high school and post-secondary education to students from across the United States.”</p><p>“We cater to students who seek an honors-level program in both religious and general education, as part of a growth-oriented community,” according to the web site. “Special emphasis is placed on ethics and character building which results in graduates of the highest moral standards.”</p><p>Founded in 2019, the school is on the edge of the Sauganash and Peterson Park neighborhoods and also offers “dormitory facilities” so “in-town and out-of-town students” have “the opportunity to focus on their studies unhindered.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-e60000" name="image-e60000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b0d141b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1182x663+0+322/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F97%2F79%2Fe2b7bf9f4a6cad97231c8111ead6%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-02-at-2-52-29-pm.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5de5671/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1182x663+0+322/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F97%2F79%2Fe2b7bf9f4a6cad97231c8111ead6%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-02-at-2-52-29-pm.png 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3d97222/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1182x663+0+322/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F97%2F79%2Fe2b7bf9f4a6cad97231c8111ead6%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-02-at-2-52-29-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Some of the allegations contained in a recently settled lawsuit against a Chicago rabbi and a Jewish school in New York where, as a teenager, he was accused of sexually assaulting his high school dormitory roommate. Both are identified under pseudonyms in the case." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3d97222/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1182x663+0+322/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F97%2F79%2Fe2b7bf9f4a6cad97231c8111ead6%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-02-at-2-52-29-pm.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e28f2de/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1182x663+0+322/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F97%2F79%2Fe2b7bf9f4a6cad97231c8111ead6%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-02-at-2-52-29-pm.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3d97222/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1182x663+0+322/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F97%2F79%2Fe2b7bf9f4a6cad97231c8111ead6%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-02-at-2-52-29-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Some of the allegations contained in a recently settled lawsuit against a Chicago rabbi and a Jewish school in New York where, as a teenager, he was accused of sexually assaulting his high school dormitory roommate. Both are identified under pseudonyms in the case.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The lawsuit, filed by the accuser under the pseudonym John Doe, accused the rabbi, when they were teens, of sexually abusing him “regularly and repeatedly . . . at least 300 times over the course of two years,” court records show.</p><p>The suit also named Torah High School, also known as Mesivta of Long Beach, and two rabbis there as defendants, saying they either knew the accuser was being sexually abused or should have.</p><p>The school “completely controlled what the children did hour by hour,” yet nobody there “supervised, checked on or even determined if the children were healthy and well from day to day,” the lawsuit said.</p><p>The suit said that during one “bed check,” a Torah High School leader walked in while the future rabbi was victimizing the accuser in their room and “did nothing to stop the abuse,” instead admonishing that he did not want to “see that again.”</p><p>While the suit was still active, attorneys for the New York school tried to shield the Torah rabbis from being named, and also tried to force the accuser to use his real name — prompting the accuser’s lawyer to allege that the defendants were trying to harass and shame him.</p><p>The case isn’t an anomaly, says Ariella Kay of ZA'AKAH, a group that advocates for survivors of sexual violence in the Jewish community.</p><p>Child sex abuse is relatively common in Orthodox Jewish communities, but it’s often swept under the rug or otherwise not publicly addressed, Kay said, calling Jewish communities “20 years behind” some other faith groups in dealing with this scourge.</p><p>Publicizing cases is important not only because it helps victims on their “healing journey,” they also serve as “cautionary tales for the community about what happens when you don’t deal with this properly.”</p><p>There have been other child sex allegations — and alleged cover-ups — centering on Chicago’s West Rogers Park neighborhood where there’s a sizable Orthodox Jewish community, records show.</p><p>The New York lawsuit alleges there “were other instances of sexual abuse that occurred” at the Torah High School.</p><p>That case was sealed by the court in recent months. Before that occurred, a court filing revealed a 2019 letter said to be from the Yeshiva Eitz Chaim teacher to his accuser in which he said:</p><p>“I would like to apologize for what happened when we were together in 11th grade. As I got older, I realized the severity of what I’ve done. My words cannot undo my actions, but I would like to express my deepest regret and apology.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/09/30/north-side-rabbi-jewish-school-yeshiva-ohr-boruch-veitzener-cheder-west-rogers-park-dovid-akiva-shenkman" target="_blank" >Rabbi overseeing Jewish elementary school asked judge to go easy on man guilty of child porn</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Robert Herguth

entries.title: Some Cook County Board commissioners skip more than 20% of their meetings
entries.summary: <p>Four incumbent Cook County commissioners — including two county board members facing Democratic primary challenges on March 17 — each missed more than 20% of the meetings they were supposed to be at since the beginning of the current term, a WBEZ analysis of county records has found.</p><p>The station compiled and analyzed the attendance records from 800 public meetings since the four-year term began in December 2022, finding that the sitting commissioners who did not show up with the greatest frequency were Stanley Moore, Bridget Gainer and Kisha McCaskill.</p><p>While Moore is running unopposed for re-election, Gainer and McCaskill have to overcome primary foes to win another term, and their challengers were eager to attempt to capitalize on the results of the WBEZ analysis of meeting attendance reports.</p><p>“I think any commissioner who’s able needs to be attending,” said Gainer challenger Drake Warren. “Showing up for work is a really important part of the job.”</p><p>WBEZ’s analysis covered county board meetings and board committee sessions and also all gatherings of the Cook County Forest Preserve board – which consists of the elected county commissioners – and Forest Preserve committee meetings during the ongoing term. The overall attendance rate was nearly 87%, the station’s review found.</p><p>But several commissioners fell far short of that mark.</p><p>Moore was reported absent at 131 of 487 meetings he was supposed to attend over the three years and three months since the county board was sworn in for a new term in early December 2022, records show. That represented an absentee rate of nearly 27%.</p><p>Moore historically had not missed many meetings in the years after taking office in 2013, but he acknowledged being absent frequently from board sessions in the last three years because, he said, he was the primary caregiver for three close family members with serious illnesses.</p><p>In an interview Thursday, Moore said he still was effective for his constituents, citing improvements to the Forest Preserve facilities in the Dan Ryan Woods.</p><p>“Because of the work I’ve done, nobody is running against me,” Moore said.</p><p>Among incumbent board members, Gainer had the second-worst record, missing nearly 23% of her meetings. But that represented an improvement compared to her truancy rate between 2013 and 2018, when an investigation by the Chicago Sun-Times revealed Gainer had <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2018/1/24/18431895/4-cook-county-board-members-missed-25-or-more-meetings-in-past-5-years-survey" target="_blank" ><u>missed nearly a third</u></a> of all the meetings she was expected to be present for during that five-year period.</p><p>In a statement Thursday, Gainer said she made it to almost all the gatherings of the full board this term, but she was absent from many lower-profile meetings.</p><p>“Attendance is an important part of the job,” she said. “I know I have room for improvement with committees and Forest Preserves, and I’ll continue to strive to do so.”</p><p>She said she had “delivered real results” in her board district, including “creating 3,000 units of affordable housing.”</p><p>But her challenger, Warren, noted that Gainer — who has been on the county board <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/eight-forty-eight/2009/04/13/bridget-gainer-replaces-quigley-as-commissioner" target="_blank" ><u>since 2009</u></a> — has long been notorious for missing a relatively large proportion of official meetings. The 2018 investigation by the Sun-Times found Gainer also had a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2018/2/11/18362599/2-cook-county-commissioners-missed-over-a-third-of-forest-preserve-meetings" target="_blank" ><u>poor attendance record </u></a>at Forest Preserve board meetings.</p><p>Warren said Gainer’s absenteeism relative to other commissioners was part of his motivation to run against her. If elected, he promised to have “stellar attendance” and not work at another job outside of being a county commissioner, which pays a salary of $102,170 a year in 2026, according to county budget records.</p><p>In addition to being a county commissioner, Gainer works for Aon, the global professional services firm with North American headquarters in downtown Chicago’s Aon Center.</p><p>McCaskill, who has touted her long tenure as park district executive director for south suburban Harvey, was appointed to the county board in January 2025 to fill the 5th District vacancy created by the election of Monica Gordon to county clerk.</p><p>In the first 13 months after joining the board, McCaskill was supposed to be at 201 meetings but was absent 43 times, according to the minutes from those sessions. That represented an absenteeism rate of 21%.</p><p>As she runs for what would be her first full term, McCaskill first must overcome a primary challenge from Dolton Trustee Kiana Belcher.</p><p>McCaskill did not return messages, but Belcher said she was concerned about McCaskill missing meetings in her first year in office. The challenger promised she would not work any other job if elected to be a commissioner.</p><p>“Taxpayers will have my full and undivided attention,” Belcher said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-9e0000" name="html-embed-module-9e0000"></a><div class="flourish-embed flourish-chart" data-src="visualisation/27918915"><script src="https://public.flourish.studio/resources/embed.js"></script><noscript><img src="https://public.flourish.studio/visualisation/27918915/thumbnail" width="100%" alt="visualization" /></noscript></div> </div> </div><p>Unlike the last time a media investigation gauged the truancy rate among commissioners, though, the county board now considers many of the absences it recorded to be “excused.” Without any public debate, at the first meeting of the current term, commissioners unanimously approved a <a class="Link" href="https://library.municode.com/il/cook_county/codes/code_of_ordinances?nodeId=PTIGEOR_CH2AD_ARTIIICOBO_DIV2RUORPR_S2-103QUREMEBOCOJOCOSU" target="_blank" ><u>one-paragraph amendment</u></a> to their rules that allows them to call in ahead of time to be marked down as excused.</p><p>The rule does not require the commissioners to put their excuse in writing or to even state the reason for the absence, although the change did stipulate that excused absences can be taken for reasons such as “illness, family emergency.”</p><p>For its analysis, WBEZ considered commissioners absent when records reflected that the absence was excused.</p><p>The other incumbents who fell short of the board’s average attendance rate were Sean Morrison (20%), Kevin Morrison (20%), Josina Morita (19%) and Donna Miller (15%), according to county records.</p><p>Sean Morrison, the lone Republican on the 17-member county board, is not running for another term. Neither are Kevin Morrison and Miller, who are running for seats in Congress in the primary this month.</p><p>In a statement Thursday, Kevin Morrison said he completed law school and prepared for the bar exam during this term “because being an attorney better equips me to serve on the Cook County Board.” He also said he had to go to Florida “to support my fiance, whose parents required end-of-life care.”</p><p>Despite those challenges, he said, he took credit for updates to the county’s human rights ordinance, advocated for “due process rights for Cook County citizens from outrageous ICE agents” and held public hearings on human trafficking and anti-trans violence.</p><p>“I remain deeply committed to my work as commissioner representing the communities of the 15th District and will remain that through the remainder of my term,” Kevin Morrison said.</p><p>Miller also said she was absent on meeting days at times for valid personal and professional reasons, including taking part in the Electoral College in Springfield in 2024, attending a national conference of Black county officials, a hospitalization and the death of her stepfather.</p><p>Some of the times when Miller was absent, she was marked as missing several meetings that were all held on the same day.</p><p>“I’m on the most committees of any commissioner,” she said. “Other [board commissioners] are absent a lot. I take it very seriously. All of my absences were excused, and all were for legitimate reasons.”</p><p>Morita said she and her staff found multiple instances where she said the minutes were incorrect — she was marked absent on the minutes even though she was at those meetings.</p><p>The lowest attendance record during the current county board was recorded by Commissioner Dennis Deer — but he was severely ill for an extended period before dying while still in office in 2024. Deer suffered serious health issues after contracting COVID-19 in 2022.</p><p>One commissioner who left the county board in the middle of his term — to become mayor of Chicago — missed more than two dozen meetings in the first few months after the 2022 board election. Brandon Johnson racked up 26 absences out of 62 meetings, for a 42% absence rate, between being sworn in for the current term and getting elected mayor a few months later, in the 2023 city election.</p><p>With a 99% mark, the best attendance rate this term belonged to Anthony Quezada, who skipped just three of 399 meetings before leaving the county board to become the Chicago City Council member for the 35th Ward last year.</p><p>The incumbent with the best attendance rate was Maggie Trevor, from Arlington Heights, with a 98.5% mark. She was present for 590 of 599 meetings. Nobody filed to challenge Trevor’s re-election bid.</p><p>Veteran Commissioner John Daley, the chairman of the board’s Finance Committee, also continued his historically stellar attendance with a 94% mark for this term. Daley was at the board 99% of the time between 2013 and 2018.</p><p>“I think it’s key. You’re elected to represent your district,” said Daley, the brother and son of former Chicago mayors.</p><p>Daley said he was well aware of and supported the rule change to allow for excused absences, pointing out that he missed being at the county board only when he was sick and for his sister’s funeral.</p><p>“I don’t think commissioners are misusing this,” Daley said of the rule change. “If they are, they should know better.”</p><p>Besides sickness or family issues, Daley said, there was little reason to miss meetings, given that the sessions are scheduled well ahead of time: “Back in November or December of the previous year, for the entire year.”</p><p><i>Dan Mihalopoulos is an investigative reporter for WBEZ. Alden Loury is data projects editor. Amy Qin is a data reporter.</i></p><h3>READ OUR PREVIOUS WATCHDOGS REPORT</h3><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a
