entries.title: Goodbye, chef: 'The Bear' to end after upcoming fifth season, report says
entries.summary: <p>“The Bear” is coming to an end, according to reporting from <a class="Link" href="https://deadline.com/2026/03/the-bear-ending-season-5-fx-1236744166/" target="_blank" ><u>Deadline</u></a>, which says the upcoming fifth season of FX’s Chicago-based kitchen dramedy will be the last.</p><p>While fans had speculated this could be the final season, chatter grew after recurring guest star Jamie Lee Curtis <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/p/DU3u0NGEYe2/" target="_blank" ><u>posted a picture on Instagram</u></a> on Feb. 17 with Abby Elliott, who plays her on-screen daughter, and a caption that starts with: "FINISHED STRONG!” On a red carpet this week, Curtis doubled down, telling <a class="Link" href="https://www.etonline.com/media/videos/jamie-lee-curtis-on-if-the-bear-is-really-ending-and-taking-on-murder-she-wrote-240415" target="_blank" ><u>Entertainment Tonight</u></a>, “It is the end of the show, everybody knows it's the end of the show. I'm not breaking any news to anybody.”</p><p>FX has not confirmed the series is ending. In recent weeks, the show has been spotted across town, with filming notices using the alias “<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/movies-and-tv/2026/02/11/the-bear-hulu-chicago-restaurants-filming-locations-ebon-moss-bachrach-matty-mattheson-ukrainian-village" target="_blank" ><u>The Fugitive</u></a>” posted in Ukrainian Village and outside Armitage Alehouse in Lincoln Park. (It’s not the first time the highly popular show has tried to <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/culture-the-arts/2024/05/02/the-bear-theories-about-code-name-kubelik-used-in-chicago" target="_blank" ><u>conceal its identity</u></a>.) The latest season is expected to drop in June, just as the others have done consecutively since 2022.</p><p>Over five seasons, the show put a spotlight on Chicago’s dining scene and made stars out of its ensemble of actors, which includes Jeremy Allen White, Ayo Edebiri, Ebon Moss-Bachrach, Liza Colón-Zayas, Lionel Boyce and Matty Matheson. It also featured cameos from the likes of Bob Odenkirk, John Mulaney, Olivia Colman and plenty of real-life Chicago chefs.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“The Bear” has propelled its cast, including White, Ayo Edebiri and Ebon Moss-Bachrach to stardom.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Chris Pizzello/Invision/Associated Press</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>As the show filmed across town, spotting the actors in the wild became a hobby for local fans, with stars seen both in and out of character on city streets, at Wrigley Field and inside restaurants like Kasama.</p><p>Created by suburban native Christopher Storer, the plot follows Carmy Berzatto (White) as he returns home to Chicago to take over his family’s Italian beef stand and turn it into a fine dining destination.</p><p>After the 2022 premiere of the show’s chaotic, fast-paced first season (which drew heavily on <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/culture-the-arts/2022/07/29/the-bear-tv-show-is-fueled-by-its-chicago-actors" target="_blank" ><u>Chicago actors in the supporting cast</u></a>), Chicago seemed divided on whether the show accurately portrayed the city. After all, Carmy had a 773 tattoo, which some local viewers felt implausible. But, Chicago also served as a central character of the show. Long, beautiful aerial shots of the CTA and the lakefront, plus a soundtrack that featured local music and talents like Lin Brehmer, served as a love letter to the city’s beautiful grittiness.</p><p>“It really couldn’t be made anywhere else; it just wouldn’t be the same project,” Jonah Zeiger, then-head of the Chicago Film Office, <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/culture-the-arts/2024/02/23/the-bear-season-three-filming-in-chicago-release-date" target="_blank" ><u>told WBEZ in 2024</u></a>.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7535d32/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+160/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F16%2Fd3%2Fe8fb5a54400aac81237ad98fdb27%2Ffxs-the-bear-season-3-first-look-image-2.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7535d32/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+160/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F16%2Fd3%2Fe8fb5a54400aac81237ad98fdb27%2Ffxs-the-bear-season-3-first-look-image-2.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1ab335b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+160/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F16%2Fd3%2Fe8fb5a54400aac81237ad98fdb27%2Ffxs-the-bear-season-3-first-look-image-2.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7535d32/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+160/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F16%2Fd3%2Fe8fb5a54400aac81237ad98fdb27%2Ffxs-the-bear-season-3-first-look-image-2.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The fifth season of “The Bear” is expected to drop in June.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Chuck Hodes/FX</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>In the second season, Chicago’s real-world restaurant scene stepped into the spotlight. The third episode of that season featured chef Sydney (Edebiri) on <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2023/6/30/23779074/every-chicago-restaurant-spotted-during-sydneys-food-tour-in-the-bear" target="_blank" ><u>a food tour across town</u></a>, with stops at Avec, Pizza Lobo, Giant, Lao Peng You and Margie’s Candies.</p><p>As the show hit its stride, the second season broke a record for the <a class="Link" href="https://variety.com/2024/tv/awards/the-bear-emmys-comedy-nomination-record-1236062449/" target="_blank" ><u>most-ever Emmy nominations</u></a> by a comedy series, with 23 nominations (to date, the show has amassed 49 Emmy nominations and 21 wins).</p><p>The series’ third season ended with some serious screen time for local chefs, including Grant Achatz of Alinea, Genie Kwon of Kasama and Anna Posey of Elske. The scene brought together chefs for a final service at a fictionalized version of Ever, shot in the dining room of <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/food-drink/2025/07/31/curtis-duffy-book-fireproof-chicago-restaurants-ever-grace" target="_blank" ><u>chef Curtis Duffy</u></a>’s real Chicago restaurant of the same name.</p><p>“‘The Bear’ gets it right,” Duffy told WBEZ/Sun-Times in 2025. “There is some drama in there, and it sells, but, for the most part, they do get it right. They've studied a lot of the front of the house, the back of the house, the conversations behind the scenes, all of those are very real and relevant to someone who's in the business.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/movies-tv/2024/06/27/the-bear-hulu-season-3-chicago-restaurants-guide-filming-locations" >Discover the dozen Chicago restaurants with cameos in season three of ‘The Bear’</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/culture-the-arts/2024/03/12/the-bear-season-three-filming-locations-chicago" >Where is ‘The Bear’ filming? Finding the set is Chicago’s new favorite spectator sport.</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/culture-the-arts/2024/05/02/the-bear-theories-about-code-name-kubelik-used-in-chicago" >Sleuthing the meaning behind the code name used by TV hit ‘The Bear’</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/culture-the-arts/2022/07/29/the-bear-tv-show-is-fueled-by-its-chicago-actors" >‘The Bear’ is the breakout TV series of summer, thanks to its supporting cast</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p><i>Courtney Kueppers is an arts and culture reporter at WBEZ.</i></p>
entries.author: Courtney Kueppers | WBEZ

entries.title: It's not the fault of Bulls guard Collin Sexton for doing Sexton things
entries.summary: <p>PHOENIX — The assignment seemed to be an obvious one.</p><p>Then again, that’s where this <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls" >Bulls</a> front office continues to have a disconnect with not only a majority of their own fan base, but the rest of the NBA.</p><p>The league says the sky is blue, executive vice president of basketball operations Arturas Karnisovas insists it’s purple. The non-play-in teams say down and Karnisovas screams up.</p><p>Common sense to improve lottery odds are that a sinking team limits the playing time of a veteran guard that could very well be in another jersey come July free agency, and Karnisovas thumbs his nose at that, allowing Collin Sexton to put in 38 minutes of work in Thursday’s win over the Suns and score 30 points.</p><p>Make it make sense.</p><p>Sure, Karnisovas can minimize the odds of hitting the top pick from the No. 9 spot — where the Bulls currently reside — compared to the No. 10 spot, pointing out it’s only a 1.5% difference (3% to 4.5%), but he’s again missing the big picture.</p><p>The chance of hitting in the top four picks — the heart of this draft class — is 20.3% if the Bulls stay at No. 9. Keep messing around and moving ahead of Milwaukee at No. 10 and the odds drop to 13.9%. Be better at staying the course and run down Memphis at No. 8? The odds of hitting top four increase to 26.3%.</p><p>If this Bulls front office had any sort of track record of consistently hitting on draft picks, sure, no big deal, but they have not. This is about trying to make it a bit more dummy-proof and give them the best odds at getting talent.</p><p>Meanwhile, the one person that loves the mentality of this Bulls front office? Sexton. As well he should.</p><p>It’s not the guard’s fault he’s doing his job, nor is it the fault of coach Billy Donovan who has been given the blessing by the front office to try and win as many games as possible with this roster.</p><p>Sexton has been around long enough to know that the Bulls could have handcuffed their coach and told him not to play the experienced guys, especially late in close games. Coming over from Charlotte in the Coby White deal, Sexton could easily be withering on the bench, counting down the days until free agency.</p><p>“Definitely very appreciative,” Sexton said of his showcase game against the Suns being allowed to happen. “At the end of the day I always take one day at a time, one second at a time, one minute at a time. At the end of the day I always tell myself be where my feet are at and be grounded and focus on the now. Don’t focus too far in the future.</p><p>“I appreciate the team as well as the organization giving me the opportunity and trusting me down the stretch even with the ball, but also to make plays for others. I appreciate that.”</p><p>And Donovan appreciates him.</p><p>In Sexton, the coach has a hard-nosed, emotional leader that is learning to play the high-paced tempo in which Donovan wants his offense to run. And he’s done so with no push back. But even if there are plans to keep Sexton beyond the next five weeks, there are still ways that they should be finessing the standings.</p><p>Just don’t include Sexton in those plans, as he remains focused on winning games and building his relationship with Donovan.</p><p>“I appreciate Coach,” Sexton added. “He’s been going through a lot (the death of his father and mother-in-law in the last month) and I’ve been praying for him, my family has been praying for him. And for him to come out each and every day with a smile on his face and trying to encourage us … he’s had a lot going on.”</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module entries.author: Joe Cowley

entries.title: Las escuelas Aspira pronto no tendrán maestros ni dinero y CPS navega para cerrarlas
entries.summary: <p>Las Escuelas Públicas de Chicago (CPS) se encuentran atrapadas en un dilema mientras intentan trasladar a los estudiantes de una red de escuelas chárter que, hasta ahora, se ha negado a cerrar oficialmente, a pesar de que se quedará sin dinero el próximo mes.</p><p>La situación sin precedentes está <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/02/27/mas-de-500-estudiantes-se-veran-desplazados-por-el-cierre-de-las-secundarias-charter-aspira" target="_blank" >generando confusión para aproximadamente 545 estudiantes</a> que se enfrentan a la perspectiva de encontrar una nueva escuela justo meses antes del final del año. También ha avivado un debate sobre la financiación de las escuelas chárter y ha suscitado preguntas sobre la responsabilidad de estas instituciones.</p><p>La red de escuelas chárter Aspira envió notificaciones de despido el miércoles a unos 60 maestros y al personal, reconociendo que CPS está transfiriendo a los estudiantes de sus dos secundarias en el lado noroeste y que el distrito “no proporcionará financiación adicional para la operación de estas escuelas”.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-bc0000" name="module-bc0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Sin embargo, en esas notificaciones, los líderes de Aspira también dicen que esperan que los cierres de escuelas sean “temporales”. El director ejecutivo Edgar López le dijo a WBEZ que legalmente tuvo que enviar las notificaciones de despido, señalando que incluso recibió una él mismo. Manifestó que sigue trabajando para mantener las escuelas abiertas hasta junio.</p><p>En otra señal de que Aspira aún no se rinde: la red chárter lanzó una campaña de recaudación de fondos para intentar recaudar dinero y que los estudiantes puedan “terminar el año escolar en un entorno estable y sin interrupciones”. Pero solo ha recaudado $2,000 en los seis días desde su lanzamiento —<a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2026/02/11/cps-charter-school-cant-make-payroll-insolvent-closed-march-aspira-chiarts-cpsboard" target="_blank" >muy por debajo de los millones que Aspira ha dicho que necesita</a> para continuar pagando a los maestros durante los próximos meses.</p><p>Los funcionarios de CPS finalmente dejaron claro la semana pasada que piensan que los estudiantes deberían abandonar Aspira. Enviaron cartas a las familias informando que se les ofrecerá un lugar a los estudiantes en una escuela cercana durante el resto del año escolar. CPS también se reunió con las familias el jueves por la tarde.</p><p>Aún así, señalaron que el chárter de Aspira, que le permite operar escuelas dentro de CPS, no está siendo revocado “en este momento”.</p><h3><b>CPS dice que la ley estatal dificulta el cierre total de Aspira </b></h3><p>Los miembros de la junta escolar aprobaron el contrato más reciente de Aspira en mayo. Este va desde el 1 de julio de 2025 hasta el 30 de junio de 2027. Pero Aspira aún no ha firmado un acuerdo por escrito y CPS dice que todavía están en negociaciones con el operador chárter, así como con varios otros aprobados en mayo.</p><p>Sin embargo, CPS asegura que no pueden simplemente cerrar Aspira, independientemente de si hay un acuerdo firmado.</p><p>“La Ley de Escuelas Chárter de Illinois establece explícitamente que ‘ninguna junta escolar local puede revocar arbitrariamente o de manera caprichosa o no renovar una chárter’ y ‘la revocación debe llevarse a cabo al final de un año escolar’”, dijo un vocero de CPS a WBEZ en un correo electrónico.</p><p>A principios de esta semana, la directora ejecutiva interina/superintendente Macquline King dijo que esos términos colocan al distrito escolar en una posición complicada.</p><p>“Deben elegir cerrar la escuela por sí mismos o CPS estará en violación de su contrato”, dijo King. “Estamos caminando una línea muy delgada para responder de una manera que sea humana y aceptable y que muestre que escuchamos a todos los miembros de la comunidad que vienen ante nosotros cada día, a la vez que honramos el contrato”.</p><p>Sin embargo, el Sindicato de Maestros de Chicago (CTU), que representa a los maestros, clínicos y auxiliares de salón de Aspira, piensa que el distrito escolar debería tomar acciones más definitivas. Los funcionarios del sindicato dicen que el distrito escolar debería centrarse más en la “crisis que enfrentan los estudiantes” en lugar de en “el deseo del distrito de evitar la posibilidad de litigios”.</p><p>CTU pidió a CPS que permita a los estudiantes transferirse juntos en grupos, que designen escuelas específicas “acogedoras” que recibirán a los estudiantes de Aspira y que permitan a algunos maestros y otros empleados de Aspira seguir a sus estudiantes para que vean caras conocidas.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-1b0000" name="image-1b0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6c23f03/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+277/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fde%2F65%2F383943f44241a53d4167ec7fedf6%2Fdsc04666.jpg width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/453d6cd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+277/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fde%2F65%2F383943f44241a53d4167ec7fedf6%2Fdsc04666.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Los funcionarios de las Escuelas Públicas de Chicago dicen que la red de escuelas chárter Aspira está al borde del colapso financiero y sería imposible mantener sus dos escuelas abiertas hasta fines de junio de 2026." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/453d6cd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+277/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fde%2F65%2F383943f44241a53d4167ec7fedf6%2Fdsc04666.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6799b9f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+277/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fde%2F65%2F383943f44241a53d4167ec7fedf6%2Fdsc04666.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/453d6cd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+277/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fde%2F65%2F383943f44241a53d4167ec7fedf6%2Fdsc04666.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Los funcionarios de las Escuelas Públicas de Chicago dicen que la red de escuelas chárter Aspira está al borde del colapso financiero y sería imposible mantener sus dos escuelas abiertas hasta fines de junio de 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Mientras tanto, la Red de Escuelas Chárter de Illinois (INCS), que aboga por las escuelas chárter, dijo que CPS debería haber hecho más para evitar que Aspira se quedara sin dinero.</p><p>El grupo indicó que la inacción de CPS fue “política y no en el mejor interés de las familias de CPS”, señalando que muchos estudiantes y familias de Aspira se vieron <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/12/15/cuando-llego-inmigracion-a-chicago-los-vecinos-se-unieron-para-llevar-a-los-ninos-a-la-escuela" target="_blank" >afectados por el aumento de la aplicación de la ley de inmigración federal en sus vecindarios</a>.</p><p>“La estabilidad importa en este momento”, dijeron los funcionarios de INCS en un comunicado. “Mantener ambos campus abiertos hasta el final del año académico debería ser el enfoque y evitaría las obvias consecuencias negativas de trasladar a los estudiantes de secundaria a una escuela desconocida justo semanas antes de la graduación”.</p><p>INCS y Aspira también afirman que CPS ha hecho más difícil que las escuelas chárter operen al proporcionar muy poca financiación por estudiante y al cambiar cómo asigna a los estudiantes a las escuelas. CPS ha dicho que está proporcionando a las escuelas chárter la mayor financiación posible, cumpliendo con la ley estatal.</p><p>CPS se da cuenta cada vez más de que está limitada en cómo puede abordar las escuelas chárter con problemas.</p><p>Cuando los distritos escolares comenzaron a otorgar contratos a organizaciones privadas para administrar escuelas públicas hace 30 años, uno de los argumentos de venta era que sus contratos podían cancelarse fácilmente y que sería más fácil cerrar una escuela chárter que una administrada por el distrito.</p><p>Sin embargo, los miembros de la junta escolar se han mostrado reacios a cerrar escuelas chárter o permitir que cierren voluntariamente, ya que los datos han demostrado que los cierres de escuelas a menudo perjudican a los estudiantes. En los últimos dos años, la junta escolar de Chicago ha votado para hacerse cargo de seis escuelas chárter administradas privadamente después de que sus operadores ya no quisieron dirigirlas.</p><p>El distrito también ha tenido que enfrentarse a desafíos legales. En 2022, un juez impidió a CPS revocar su contrato con la red chárter Urban Prep, que el distrito buscó cancelar debido a los problemas financieros de Urban Prep y las acusaciones de mala conducta sexual de su entonces director ejecutivo.</p><p>Urban Prep alegó que un moratorio estatal sobre cierres de escuelas se aplicaba a las escuelas chárter y, tras una prolongada batalla legal, se le permitió seguir operando. Ese moratorio ya no está vigente, pero Urban Prep aún mantiene un contrato para administrar escuelas en CPS.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Sarah Karp | WBEZ

entries.title: Trump destituye a Kristi Noem, Pritzker dice 'que la puerta no te golpee al salir'
entries.summary: <p>Los demócratas de Illinois están celebrando la destitución de la controversial jefa del Departamento de Seguridad Nacional (DHS), Kristi Noem, meses después de que liderara agresivas operativos de inmigración en el área de Chicago, que dejaron a una persona muerta y a otra que sobrevivió luego de recibir cinco disparos por parte de un agente federal de inmigración.</p><p>“No dejes que la puerta te golpee al salir, Kristi Noem”, escribió el gobernador demócrata JB Pritzker en redes sociales. El gobernador también publicó un perfil falso de LinkedIn con la cara de Noem y una etiqueta de “#buscandoempleo”.</p><p>La representante estadounidense Delia Ramírez, quien se reunió la semana pasada con Noem y le recordó que sus días estaban contados, escribió en X: “¡Qué alivio! Aún tenemos que destituirla, desmantelar ICE y desmantelar DHS”. La representante estadounidense Robin Kelly la llamó “una líder incompetente que enriqueció a sus amigos y desató a sus agentes de Gestapo sin consideración por la ley ni por la vida humana”.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-5b0000" name="module-5b0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>El alcalde Brandon Johnson dijo que Noem “armó el miedo para socavar la confianza en las comunidades”, y añadió, “No se le extrañará en Chicago”.</p><p>“Su mandato será recordado por su corrupción descarada, la explotación irresponsable de familias vulnerables para avanzar en una agenda de extrema derecha y su disposición a poner los intereses del Presidente por encima de los del pueblo estadounidense”, dijo Johnson en un comunicado.</p><p>Hubo rumores de insatisfacción de parte del presidente Donald Trump tras dos días consecutivos de testimonios de Noem ante comités del Senado y la Cámara, que incluyeron un intenso interrogatorio tanto de demócratas como de republicanos. Trump en la red social Truth Social dijo que nominará al senador republicano de Oklahoma, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2026/03/05/markwayne-mullin-new-dhs-director-department-of-homeland-security" target="_blank" >Markwayne Mullin</a>, para reemplazarla. Trump dijo que hará de Noem una “Enviada Especial para El Escudo de las Américas”, una nueva iniciativa de seguridad que, según él, se enfocará en el Hemisferio Occidental.</p><p>Noem es la primera secretaria del gabinete en dejar su puesto durante el segundo mandato de Trump y fue la muy pública cara del plan de inmigración del presidente en comerciales destinados a reclutar agentes federales de inmigración y a instar a las personas sin estatus legal a autodeportarse. Trump destituyó al comandante de la patrulla fronteriza, Gregory Bovino, de supervisar los operativos de inmigración en Minneapolis tras los tiroteos mortales de Alex Pretti y Renee Good a manos de oficiales federales.</p><p>Bajo la dirección de Noem, al menos 2,800 personas fueron arrestadas en Illinois durante el Operativo Midway Blitz entre mediados de septiembre y mediados de octubre, principalmente en el área de Chicago, según datos del Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE) obtenidos por el Proyecto de Deportación de Datos y declaraciones sobre los arrestos de la Patrulla Fronteriza realizadas por el Departamento de Justicia en la corte. El operativo se desaceleró a mediados de noviembre.</p><p>A Silverio Villegas González, un padre de familia y cocinero originario de México, le dispararon a “quemarropa” cuando supuestamente intentó huir de una parada de tráfico el 12 de septiembre.</p><p>El senador Dick Durbin reaccionó a la salida de Noem diciendo que no era “apta para el trabajo” y la acusó de mentir bajo juramento ante el Congreso.</p><p>“Kristi Noem fue la cara de una campaña de deportación masiva impopular, ilegal y abiertamente peligrosa que cobró la vida de estadounidenses inocentes y aterrorizó a muchos más. Nuestro país expresó su indignación, ella mintió varias veces ante el Congreso e incluso el Presidente finalmente admitió que no era apta para el trabajo”, destacó Durbin.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="La secretaria de Seguridad Nacional, Kristi Noem, testifica ante el Comité Judicial del Senado el martes 2 de marzo de 2026." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/030ff85/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5599x3142+0+295/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fac%2F75%2F3bda8bcd4078a8856fb2c8094c6e%2F2264587768.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/10b0688/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5599x3142+0+295/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fac%2F75%2F3bda8bcd4078a8856fb2c8094c6e%2F2264587768.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/030ff85/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5599x3142+0+295/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fac%2F75%2F3bda8bcd4078a8856fb2c8094c6e%2F2264587768.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Javier Ramírez y Leonardo García Venegas, ciudadanos estadounidenses que fueron detenidos por ICE, y Marimar Martínez, ciudadana estadounidense a la que le disparó cinco veces un agente de la Patrulla Fronteriza en Chicago, están presentes mientras la secretaria de Seguridad Nacional, Kristi Noem, testifica ante el Comité Judicial del Senado en Washington, D.C., el martes.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Chip Somodevilla/Getty</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/03/04/durante-audience-kristi-noem-dijo-no-conocer-a-marimar-martinez-la-mujer-de-chicago-a-la-que-un-agente-de-ice-le-disparo" target="_blank" >El martes, Durbin le preguntó a Noem sobre las muertes de Pretti y Good</a> en Minneapolis, así como sobre <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/02/04/marimar-martinez-baleada-por-agentes-de-migracion-se-pronuncia-contra-asesinatos" target="_blank" >Marimar Martínez, la mujer de Chicago que sobrevivió a cinco disparos por parte de un agente federal</a> en octubre. En cada caso, el DHS etiquetó a las víctimas como “terroristas domésticos”.</p><p>“Tenemos amplias evidencias en video y testimonios de testigos que demuestran que estás equivocada. Sus declaraciones causaron un dolor inconmensurable a estas familias”, le dijo Durbin a Noem. “Déjame darte la oportunidad de hacer lo correcto. ¿Retiras estas declaraciones que identifican a estos individuos como terroristas domésticos?”.</p><p>Noem calificó los tiroteos como “situaciones trágicas” y dijo que “siempre ofrecemos nuestras condolencias a esas familias”.</p><p>La jefa de DHS dijo que estaba “dependiendo de los informes desde el terreno y de los agentes que están allí y trabajando para ser transparentes”. También afirmó que no sabía sobre el tiroteo de Martínez.</p><p>Durbin también acusó a Noem el jueves de mentir bajo juramento durante el Comité Judicial del Senado del martes, incluyendo afirmar que DHS está siguiendo órdenes judiciales, a pesar de una declaración jurada que indica que ICE ha violado más de 50 órdenes judiciales en el estado de Nueva Jersey.</p><p>También negó que DHS hubiera detenido a ciudadanos estadounidenses, lo que también es falso, destacó Durbin. Y negó que el asistente Corey Lewandowski hubiera tenido algún papel en la aprobación de contratos de DHS.</p><p>“La Secretaria hizo un juramento de decir la verdad. Pero en su lugar, falló repetidamente en hacerlo”, dijo Durbin. “...Esto debería preocupar a cada miembro de este Comité —tanto republicano como demócrata. Este Comité tiene la obligación de hacer que la Secretaria rinda cuentas. Espero discutir esto contigo y con otros miembros del Comité [sobre] cuáles son nuestras opciones”.</p><p>La senadora Tammy Duckworth reaccionó a la destitución de Noem diciendo que debería haber sucedido “hace mucho tiempo”.</p><p>“A continuación, la administración de Trump necesita trabajar con los demócratas para implementar las reformas sensatas de DHS que el pueblo estadounidense exige”, dijo Duckworth en un comunicado. “Ayudar a prevenir que más estadounidenses sean ejecutados por su propio gobierno es lo mínimo que pueden hacer”.</p><p>El representante Jesus “Chuy” García calificó a DHS como una “agencia rebelde y sin ley diseñada para pisotear nuestros derechos civiles", y dijo, "Ella merecía ser despedida, pero aún necesitamos rendición de cuentas por los crímenes cometidos durante su mandato."</p><p>El miércoles, Noem enfrentó preguntas ante el Comité Judicial de la Cámara sobre un contrato publicitario, que constó de anuncios llamativos protagonizados por Noem discutiendo la campaña de inmigración en curso de Trump. Bajo interrogatorio, Noem dijo que el presidente había aprobado la campaña publicitaria de $220 millones. Pero Trump el jueves contradijo esa afirmación, según Reuters. “Nunca supe nada de esto”, aseguró Trump.</p><p>También hubo preguntas sobre los jets privados Gulfstream que Noem utilizó, que un funcionario del departamento dijo al New York Times que eran usados por razones de seguridad. La financiación provino de la asignación de DHS de la “Grande y hermosa ley” de Trump.</p><p>La semana pasada, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/02/26/en-reunion-la-representante-delia-ramirez-exige-demandas-y-amenaza-con-destituir-a-la-jefa-de-dhs" target="_blank" >Ramírez se reunió con Noem</a>y llegó equipada con solicitudes específicas, incluyendo prohibir a los agentes federales entrar en iglesias, escuelas y hospitales. Y también le dijo sin rodeos —dos veces— que trabajaría para destituirla. También le dijo que reconocía que sus “días podrían estar contados”.</p><p>Ramírez el jueves dijo al Sun-Times que la destitución de Noem es una “victoria” para los manifestantes que se opusieron a las tácticas agresivas de los agentes federales.</p><p>“Su destitución es una victoria del pueblo para todos los que han protestado, ya sea en Minneapolis, en Chicago, en Oregón o en Carolina del Norte, alrededor del país. Es una victoria para ellos”, aseguró Ramírez. “Pero tú y yo sabemos que destituir a la jefa no cambia el hecho de que esta agencia tiene la autoridad para operar de la manera en que lo ha hecho debido a su estructura, por lo que poner al senador Mullen no va a cambiar mucho”.</p><p>A pesar de su destitución, Ramírez y otros demócratas de Illinois dicen que seguirán presionando por procesos de destitución. Los representantes estadounidenses Raja Krishnamoorthi y Robin Kelly, ambos en la carrera por el escaño de Durbin, también están presionando para destituir a Noem, con Krishnamoorthi intentando el miércoles citar también a Noem, un movimiento que fue bloqueado por los republicanos de la Cámara.</p><p>Kelly presentó artículos de destitución contra Noem que ahora cuentan con 187 coauspiciadores, incluyendo a Krishnamoorthi. “La victoria final no es sólo su destitución. No es suficiente. Estamos contentos de que haya sido removida. Creo que la gente en Chicago y en todo el país que hizo mucho trabajo, que grabó, se puso en riesgo para que personas como ella ya no estén en el liderazgo”, dijo Ramírez. “Pero ella no tiene lugar en un cargo público, y tenemos que asegurarnos de que pasemos por un proceso que aborde realmente su comportamiento poco ético y las personas que han muerto bajo su vigilancia”.</p><p><i>Contribuyó: Fran Spielman </i></p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Tina Sfondeles

entries.title: Chicago short nearly 225,000 affordable rental units for its poorest residents, report finds
entries.summary: <p>Chicago's affordable housing shortage is placing severe financial strain on low-income renters, according to a report released Thursday by Housing Action Illinois and the National Low Income Housing Coalition. </p><p>The study found only 31 affordable homes are available for rent for every 100 extremely low-income renter households in Chicago. In Illinois, it's 34 available homes for every 100 extremely low-income renter household.</p><p>Extremely low-income renters are those who have annual incomes at or below 30% of the area median income. In Chicago, that’s $35,970 for a family of four and $25,200 for an individual. Most households considered extremely low-income are because they're either working low-wage jobs, are seniors or have a disability, according to the report. </p><p>The annual <a class="Link" href="file:///Users/s.h./Downloads/gap-report_2026_english.pdf" target="_blank" >study</a> estimates the availability of affordable homes for renters at different income levels, with a focus on extremely low-income households. It also provides estimates of affordable housing needs for the U.S. and the 50 largest metropolitan areas, including Chicago. It's based on data from the 2024 American Community Survey Public Use Microdata Sample, a nationwide survey of about 3.5 million addresses.</p><p>Extremely low-income renters are most likely to be impacted by the housing shortage. And that’s especially true in Chicago, Bob Palmer, policy director at Housing Action Illinois, said. </p><p>He said Chicago’s figures don’t vary much from last year, but the data shows the region’s lack of affordable housing is persistent — especially for the lowest-income renters.</p><p>“It's a chronic problem that everyone knows about, but there hasn't been the political will to deal with it in a way that fundamentally changes the current problems,” Palmer said.</p><p>The report found no major city, or state, with an adequate supply of available affordable homes for extremely low-income renters. And Chicago, typically seen as more affordable than its coastal counterparts, was no exception.</p><p>Housing Action Illinois and the National Low Income Housing Coalition found the Chicago metro area was short 224,445 units of affordable housing for its poorest residents. It means 76% of extremely low-income renters in Chicago are spending more than half of their household income on rent. Experts say renters should typically spend no more than 30% of their monthly income on housing in order to budget for other necessities, like health care and groceries.</p><p>“You have neighborhoods that are gentrifying, where there's a lot of housing pressure,” Palmer said. “You have neighborhoods mostly on the South or West Side where there's a declining housing stock because of disinvestment in the housing [and] housing being torn down.”</p><p>The lack of affordable housing contributes to homelessness and evictions, Palmer said, as well as population loss. Illinois’ population has been growing for the last three years, according to the Illinois Policy Institute, but it was on a nine-year decline before then.</p><p>The state's growth in recent years has largely been attributed to an influx in immigration. Without immigration, Illinois’ population would have declined, according to the institute. More than 40,000 residents left Illinois last year to live in another state.</p><p>“Housing is an issue or a factor that very, very much influences the future of our state,” Palmer said.</p><p>Housing affordability remains an issue because of constraints in the private market. Much of the recent apartment development in Chicago is considered luxury or high-end because developers can't afford to build lower-rate units without government subsidies, Palmer said. </p><p>The report also found Illinois needs nearly 290,000 more homes for extremely low-income households. Expanding access to rental assistance and building deeply affordable rental homes could help ease the shortage, according to the National Low Income Housing Coalition.</p>
entries.author: Abby Miller

entries.title: Troubled Chicago police officer faces firing for brandishing gun during scuffle with Westinghouse student
entries.summary: <p>Chavez Siler wasn’t supposed to have a gun.</p><p>The Chicago cop had been stripped of his police powers for pistol-whipping a man and aiming at his head during an arrest.</p><p>Still, Siler placed his service weapon on his leg when he went to work security at Westinghouse College Prep on Nov. 16, 2021.</p><p>It slipped out as he <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2021/11/18/22789419/video-westinghouse-armed-security-guard-student-scuffle" target="_blank" >tussled with a teenager</a> in a hallway. A police investigation found he later pointed the gun at the student and said “I’ll shoot.”</p><p>The top cop is now calling for Siler’s firing after criminal charges and a lawsuit were tossed out. However, some key facts remain in dispute.</p><p>The Civilian Office of Police Accountability notably didn’t sustain an allegation that Siler pointed his firearm at the boy, as the police department had concluded.</p><p>Inspector General Deborah Witzburg’s office asked COPA to reopen the case in October to address the discrepancy, noting that a viral video of the confrontation was “consistent” with the findings in the police department’s criminal investigation.</p><p>COPA’s chief administrator, Lakenya White, refused to take another look, though. In December, she told the inspector general’s office that “COPA has already recommended significant discipline.”</p><p>Siler was previously able to hang onto his job after former police Supt. David Brown called for his firing in the earlier pistol-whipping case. The Chicago Police Board suspended him for six months instead.</p><p>As mitigating evidence, Siler told the board he worked part-time as a security guard at Westinghouse — even as he faced felony charges for the scuffle at the school.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="113" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/3QFbXeaGLlI?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" allowfullscreen title="CPS investigating scuffle between student and armed security guard at Westinghouse College Prep"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p></p><h3><b>A fight at dismissal</b></h3><p>Siler spotted a group of kids using the wrong door to pick up a food delivery after the last bell had rung at Westinghouse, according to a COPA report finalized in late September. </p><p>Siler and Sharod Grafton, who was 17 at the time, both claimed the other started making threats. Siler then grabbed Grafton, pushed him against a wall and the two started to tussle, COPA said. At one point, Siler’s gun fell to the ground. </p><p>A police investigation found that a video posted to social media showed Siler briefly pointing the gun at Grafton while holding him by the neck and threatening to shoot.</p><p>“I wish you would,” Grafton said back, according to the police.</p><p>But COPA didn’t sustain allegations that Siler choked Grafton or pointed a gun at him. The agency said those claims couldn’t be verified by the video and argued that the perceived gun-pointing was “likely incidental."</p><p>COPA ultimately sustained 10 of the 21 complaints against Siler, many of which related to the altercation. COPA's report also found he had brought his gun to the school after he was “clearly ordered not to carry a firearm or any other weapon.”</p><p>As a longtime cop and security guard, COPA said Siler “should have been well-trained and equipped to handle challenging interactions with juveniles in a calm and constructive manner.” Instead, COPA found he “engaged in conduct that escalated the situation.”</p><p>The agency called for anywhere from a 365-day suspension up to discharge, and Chicago police Supt. Larry Snelling later said he’d seek Siler’s dismissal.</p><p>The inspector general’s office asked COPA to reopen the case in October, but White declined and said her agency’s work wasn’t “materially deficient.”</p><p>White argued that reopening the case “risks the viability of COPA’s sustained findings and penalty recommendation,” citing the length of the investigation.</p><h3><b>Another investigation</b></h3><p>Siler has faced dismissal before.</p><p>Back in 2021, then-Supt. Brown <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2022/4/11/23015980/copa-firing-recommended-police-officers-arrest-convenience-store-pistol-whipped-taser-humboldt-park" target="_blank" >sought to have him fired</a> over a struggle with an armed man at a mini-mart in Humboldt Park.</p><p>In March 2017, Siler placed a gun to the man’s head and repeatedly struck him with it, according to the police board. He also urged another officer to shock the man’s face with a Taser while he was being kneeled on.</p><p>The aggressive tactics only ended when the man was left “motionless on the ground,” according to COPA.</p><p>In the end, the board found some of Siler’s actions to be justified and chose not to fire him. All but one member voted to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2023/4/20/23692177/chicago-cop-who-faced-dismissal-over-role-in-violent-arrest-is-suspended-for-6-months " target="_blank" >suspend him for six months</a> without pay.</p><p>Siler’s attorneys didn’t respond to request for comment.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2021/11/18/22789419/video-westinghouse-armed-security-guard-student-scuffle" target="_blank" >CPS investigating scuffle between student and armed security guard at Westinghouse College Prep</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2022/4/11/23015980/copa-firing-recommended-police-officers-arrest-convenience-store-pistol-whipped-taser-humboldt-park" target="_blank" >Chicago cops face firing 5 years after man was ‘pistol-whipped,’ shocked with Taser during gun arrest</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2023/4/20/23692177/chicago-cop-who-faced-dismissal-over-role-in-violent-arrest-is-suspended-for-6-months" target="_blank" >Chicago cop who faced dismissal over role in violent arrest is suspended for 6 months</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Sophie Sherry

entries.title: Read Barack Obama's eulogy for Rev. Jesse Jackson
entries.summary: <p><b>Barack Obama</b>: It’s good to be home.</p><p><b>Audience</b>: We love you!</p><p><b>Obama</b>: I love you back.</p><p>The book…the book of Isaiah…</p><p><b>Obama</b>: Where he at?</p><p>(Audience crosstalk) Four more years!</p><p><b>Obama</b>: No, see I believe in the Constitution.</p><p>The book of Isaiah, God is looking for a messenger to guide a hardened and resistant people. The Lord asks, “Whom shall I send, and who will go with us?” To which Isaiah replies, “Here I am, Lord! Send me.”</p><p>To Mother Jackson and the Jackson family, to President and Mrs. Biden, President and Secretary Clinton, Vice President Harris, Pastor Dates — my old friend — though he’s looking good, Reverend Meeks, Reverend Jenkins.</p><p>It is an honor to join you today to celebrate the Reverend Jesse Louis Jackson, a man who, when the poor and the dispossessed needed a champion and the country needed healing, stepped forward again and again and again and said, “Send me.”</p><p>Reverend Jackson’s immense gifts were apparent at an early age, even if his circumstances conspired to try to hold him back. Born out of wedlock to a teenage mom, growing up under the oppressive cloud of segregation, confined to schools, sports facilities, movie theatres that were separate and unequal. There was a world where, on Thanksgiving, he’d have to wait for his mother to come home on the bus, carrying leftovers from the dinner she had to cook for somebody else; a world designed to tell a child that he or she could only go so far, that to think otherwise would be foolish or dangerous, and that wisdom required you to accept your lot in life.</p><p>Young Jesse refused to accept that verdict.</p><p>He was a born leader, an athlete, a talker — knew how to talk. Star quarterback, student body president. He could have succeeded within the confines that were determined for him and had a successful life. But like so many of his generation — so many extraordinary civil rights leaders in the late Fifties and Sixties — that Joshua generation — he instinctively understood that individual success meant nothing unless everybody was free. So he became inspired by the bus boycotts in Montgomery. Led seven Black students into the whites-only library, sitting down and getting arrested for reading. Think about that. The library closed, but then it reopened–and when it did, it was open to everyone.</p><p>Send me, Jesse said, even as a young man — and the world got a little bit better.</p><p>By the time Reverend Jackson graduated from college, he had attended the Chicago Theological Seminary, he knew the nature of his calling. And he became, as everybody knows, the youngest member of the S.C.L.C., assigned to lead Operation Breadbasket here in Chicago. Right around the time that Isaiah Thomas is talking about. And it was during this period, and especially after Dr. King’s death, when the optimism of the early movement had begun to fade and leadership had begun to fracture, and when the country seemed to have grown bored, gotten weary of the idea of justice and equality, and moved on to other concerns. That Reverend Jackson rose above despair and kept that righteous flame alive.</p><p>Through Operation PUSH, he led boycotts and challenged corporate policies around hiring and contracting, recognizing that civil rights without economic justice was an empty promise. He backed unions in their organizing efforts and activists in fair wage campaigns, understanding that if the have-nots and the have-little bits ever learned to make common cause across racial lines instead of fighting each other over bread crumbs, everybody would benefit. He helped register millions of voters, he fought against biases in the criminal justice system. He drew attention to local abuses of power, and called folks at the national level to account. And by the early Eighties, he was delivering that message of change and hope across the globe, freeing hostages from captivity, fighting to end apartheid in South Africa.</p><p>And then, in 1984, as the powers that be in Washington were rolling back hard-won progress and slashing the social safety net, when more and more folks were getting left behind, and greed was being trumpeted as a virtue. See we’ve been there before…He stepped forward, once again — and said, send me. He ran for the presidency of the United States of America.</p><p>I had just graduated from college during that first campaign. And I was living in New York at the time, I was working to pay off my student loans. Eating a lot of tuna fish and Campbell’s soup. And if I went to a diner, I’d grab some extra crackers and put them in my pocket.</p><p>And I was inspired by the civil rights movement, and I had in my mind to work for social justice. But even though I was full of good intentions, I was uncertain of how to serve and fighting off self-doubt.</p><p>And I remember how, at the time, plenty of people — including — I’m sorry — plenty of Black folks — were dismissing Jesse’s chances. Suggesting, oh, he just wants attention. He could only get Black votes. But then I remember one night, sitting in my janky apartment, and I got an old black and white TV with the rabbit ears, and I’m kind of jiggering them around. It’s about this big. I know young people can’t imagine, but the TV was about this big. And I’m watching a Democratic primary debate between him, and Walter Mondale, and Gary Hart. And I remember how, when that debate was over, I turned off that TV and I thought the same thing that I know a lot of people thought that night — even if they didn’t want to admit it. That in his ideas, in his platform, in his analysis, in his intelligence, in his insights, Jesse hadn’t just held his own — he had owned that stage. He wasn’t an intruder, he wasn’t a pretender — he belonged on that stage. And the message he sent to a 22-year-old child of a single mother, with a funny name — an outsider — was that maybe there wasn’t any place, any room, where we didn’t belong.</p><p>And that message, of fairness and dignity, of justice and hope, that’s what the Rainbow Coalition was all about. In 1984 and then again in 1988, Jesse didn’t just speak to Black folks, he spoke to to white folks and Latinos, Asian Americans and the first Americans, he spoke to family farmers and environmentalists, he spoke to gay rights activists — when nobody was talking to gay rights activists — and blue-collar workers — and he gave them the same message that they mattered. That their voices and their votes counted, he invited them to believe — he invited us to believe in our own power to change America for the better.</p><p>By the delegate count, Jesse’s two candidacies ultimately came up short. But he paved the road for so many others to follow. Doug Wilder became the first elected Black governor. Carol Moseley Braun went to the U.S. Senate. Because of Jesse, the Democratic party changed its rules, ending the winner-take-all distribution of delegates during presidential primaries, which meant underdogs and outsiders like Bill Clinton or Bernie Sanders could stay competitive and build momentum instead of getting knocked out early.</p><p>And it was because of that path that he had laid, because of his courage, his audacity, that two decades later, a young Black senator from Chicago’s South Side would even be taken seriously as a candidate for the presidential nomination.</p><p>I still credit that first run of Jesse’s, and Harold Washington’s campaign, for drawing me to Chicago. I didn’t know anybody when I first arrived, and I was working as a community organizer — as it so happens, in neighborhoods right around here — Roseland, Woodlawn, Altgeld. West Pullman. I meeting these young preachers like Meeks and some crazy priest named Pflager, and I guess Jenkins was running around, but he’s kinda young — he came a little bit later. So, I’m going to churches, and I’m listening to these amazing preachers. And it was hard work and half the time I didn’t know what I was doing, and progress was slow, and I definitely didn’t know how I was going to survive these Chicago winters because I grew up in Hawaii.</p><p>But I do remember heading to the PUSH headquarters on some Saturday mornings to listen and learn from Reverend Jackson.</p><p>And y’all remember how, on some mornings if there was a major issue going on, the room would be packed to the rafters. And sometimes, some celebrity just wanders in. And then some mornings, it was kind of lowkey. Either way though, the announcements would be made, and Reverend Barrow would say a prayer, and then Jesse would sit there, and he’d stand, and he’d start kind of slow. And he’d talk kinda low so everybody would kind of lean in — and over time it was with that same boundless energy, that would emerge, and that same passion and insight. And he’d offer you a master class in economics on a Saturday morning. He’d give you a seminar on American history and American politics. He’d make complicated things plain, and he’d tell stories that made you laugh one minute and cry the next.</p><p>And whether you were a bus driver, or a teacher, or a business leader, or a young organizer — you came away from those meetings with a better understanding of how the world worked. How power worked. And more importantly, he made you believe that if we came together, we could make the world work better.</p><p>Now we’ve been talking about how Jesse telling us “we are somebody”… “I am somebody,” and he wasn’t just talking to young Black boys like Isaiah — though he loved them fiercely — and he wasn’t just talking to young Black girls, to help them believe, though he believed in them. He was talking about everyone who was left out, everyone who was forgotten, everyone who was unseen, everyone who was unheard. And in that sense, he was expressing the very essence of what our democracy should be, the ideals at the very heart of the American experiment — the belief that regardless of what we look like or how we worship, regardless of where our ancestors came from or how much money we’ve got, we’re all part of the American family. We’re all endowed with the same inalienable rights to life and liberty and the pursuit of happiness. And we’re all obligated to answer the call, and step forward and take responsibility for making wrongs right and for caring for our neighbors and bringing the reality of America a step closer to its glorious ideals.</p><p>Answering that call isn’t always easy. And Reverend Jackson and his family knew that better than most. To do what he did, he would endure all kinds of hatred and setbacks, and betrayals, and doubters and death threats. He would sacrifice, as Jim Reynolds pointed out, the leisure and comfort of what was available to him as a more financially secure life, and those sacrifices were not his alone — they were shared by his wife and his children, and they bore that burden with grace and strength.</p><p>I got to know the Reverend and his family over the years, and as I watched his children follow his example of service, I came to appreciate the conviction that drove him, that he had passed on — the faith that guided him that he had passed on. It was a faith that did not waver; a flame that burned bright, even as his body began to fail.</p><p>The last time he and I had a chance to visit in person, he was already ailing, it was getting difficult for him to stand and difficult for him to speak. We were in Hyde Park in a hotel room and I embraced him…and figured we’d just have a low key visit, maybe he’d need some rest. And he starts coming up with this project, and this initiative, and issues I needed to look into, and here’s some commentary that he would suggest I and here’s some phrasing that he thought might work, and maybe we might co-write an article, and listening to him, I couldn’t help smiling, because it took me back. And I started reminiscing about those Saturday mornings at PUSH and the breakfast he’d host away from full meeting. I didn’t get to go to that until I was a State Senator and probably Meeks invited me… so I was tagging along with Meeks. And he’d pull out a piece a paper from his jacket pocket and start passing out assignments, and pull together working groups, and ask for updates on the latest campaign. And I’ll admit though those sessions could run a little long and did not always follow Roberts Rules of Order, but when Jesse called your name and acknowledged you in that room, especially if you were young, especially if you were just coming up, especially if you weren’t sure you could pull off what you thought you might be able to do, especially when you needed help… you stood up a little straighter, you knew that you’d been seen by this giant, he’d laid hands on you and let you know that you had a contribution to make. And so that day I told him how much that meant to me, and how much it had meant to so many whose lives that he impacted, and I told him thank you, that I would always be grateful for that legacy of hope.</p><p>I know I’ve gone long. It was going to be much shorter until Isaiah spoke. I figure if a Detroit Piston could take up this much time…it was beautiful. And I mean that. Love me some Isaiah.</p><p>We are living in a time when it can be hard to hope. Each day we wake up to some new assault on our democratic institutions, another setback to the idea of the rule of law. An offense to common decency. Every day you wake up to it, to things you just didn’t think were possible. Each day, we’re told by those in high office to fear each other and to turn on each other — and that some Americans count more than others, and that some don’t even count at all. Everywhere we see greed and bigotry being celebrated, and bullying and mockery masquerading as strength; we see science and expertise denigrated while ignorance and dishonesty, and cruelty and corruption, are reaping untold rewards. Every single day we see that. And it’s hard to hope in those moments.</p><p>So it may be tempting to get discouraged, to give in to cynicism. It may be tempting for some to compromise with power and grab what you can, or even for good people, to maybe just put your head down and wait for the storm to pass.</p><p>But this man — Reverend Jesse Louis Jackson — inspires us to take the harder path. His voice calls on each of us to be heralds of change, to be messengers of hope; to step forward and say “Send me” wherever we have a chance to make an impact — whether it’s in our schools, in our workplaces, in our neighborhoods and our cities. Not for fame, not for glory, or because success is guaranteed, but because it gives our life purpose, because it aligns with what our faith tells us God demands, and because if we don’t step up, no one else will.</p><p>How fortunate we were that Jesse Jackson answered that call. What a great debt we owe to him.</p><p>May God bless Reverend Jackson, and may he rest in eternal peace. God bless you."<br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times staff

entries.title: Bears agree to deal for Patriots C Garrett Bradbury
entries.summary: <p>The day the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> officially put center Drew Dalman on the reserve/retired list, they agreed to trade for someone to take his place.</p><p>The price was right — the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/halas-intrigue/2026/03/05/halas-intrigue-podcast-breaking-down-bears-dj-moore-trade-nfl" target="_blank" >Bears</a> are sending just a fifth-round pick in the 2027 draft for the Patriots’ Garrett Bradbury, sources confirmed to the Sun-Times on Friday night. The trade can’t be made official until the start of the league year Wednesday.</p><p>Bradbury started all 17 games for the AFC champions last year after serving as the Vikings’ starting center for his first six seasons.</p><p>He worked alongside Patriots quarterback Drake Maye, who narrowly lost the NFL MVP vote to the Rams’ Matthew Stafford. Maye, who was drafted two spots after the Bears took Caleb Williams, made a monumental leap in his second season, leading the Patriots to the Super Bowl.</p><p>Bradbury has an established friendship with one of the Bears’ most respected players — he and left guard Joe Thuney were teammates on the road when they both attended N.C. State.</p><p>His salary is reasonable, too. Bradbury, who turns 31 in June, is entering the second year of a two-year, $9.6 million contract he signed with the Patriots a year ago. He commands a $5.7 million salary cap hit this year.</p><p>His signing solves an immediate need after Dalman decided earlier this week to retire. It doesn’t necessarily preclude the Bears from looking at center in the draft; the best players at that position figure to go off the board in the third and fourth round.</p><p>Bradbury was the Vikings’ first round pick, No. 18 overall, in 2019. He’s one of the league’s smallest centers, but, like Dalman, his agility is a good match for Ben Johnson’s system.</p><p>Trading for Bradbury comes at a more modest price than paying the top available center on the market. The Ravens’ Tyler Linderbaum is projected to command a contract in the four-year, $70 million range in free agency, according to Spotrac.com.</p><p>The Bears’ newfound cap flexibility — the team saved about $44 million after Dalman’s retirement, their trade of receiver DJ Moore and the release of linebacker Tremaine Edmunds — can go toward other positions of need such as defensive line, linebacker and safety. The Bears want to re-sign Pro Bowl safety Kevin Byard and could be contenders for defensive end Maxx Crosby were the Raiders to decide to trade him. </p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH"
